quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Derivative definition"?

options.text: A relation where y is defined implicitly by an equation F(x,y) = 0 rather than explicitly as y = f(x)
f'(x) = lim(h→0) [f(x+h) - f(x)]/h; the instantaneous rate of change of f with respect to x
Local min: concave up valley (f'' > 0); local max: concave down hill (f'' < 0)
The derivative of the natural log; note that d/dx[ln|x|] = 1/x for x ≠ 0

quiz_questions.explanation: "Derivative definition" is defined as: f'(x) = lim(h→0) [f(x+h) - f(x)]/h; the instantaneous rate of change of f with respect to x. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Difference quotient"?

options.text: The derivative of the natural log; note that d/dx[ln|x|] = 1/x for x ≠ 0
[f(x+h) - f(x)]/h; the average rate of change over an interval of width h; becomes the derivative as h→0
d/dx[f(x)/g(x)] = [f'(x)g(x) - f(x)g'(x)] / [g(x)]²; 'low d-high minus high d-low over low squared'
From a graph, check if the function approaches the same value from the left and right; if not, the two-sided limit does not exist

quiz_questions.explanation: "Difference quotient" is defined as: [f(x+h) - f(x)]/h; the average rate of change over an interval of width h; becomes the derivative as h→0. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Tangent line"?

options.text: The line that touches the curve at exactly one point locally and has slope equal to the derivative: y - f(a) = f'(a)(x - a)
lim[f ± g] = lim f ± lim g; lim[f·g] = lim f · lim g; lim[f/g] = lim f / lim g (if lim g ≠ 0); lim[fⁿ] = (lim f)ⁿ
Finding a specific antiderivative by using a condition like F(0) = 3 to determine the value of C
F(x) = ∫ₐˣ f(t)dt; represents the accumulated area from a to x; its derivative is f(x) by FTC Part 1

quiz_questions.explanation: "Tangent line" is defined as: The line that touches the curve at exactly one point locally and has slope equal to the derivative: y - f(a) = f'(a)(x - a). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Differentiability"?

options.text: A relation where y is defined implicitly by an equation F(x,y) = 0 rather than explicitly as y = f(x)
Uses the midpoint of each subinterval: M_n = Σf((xᵢ + xᵢ₊₁)/2)Δx; generally more accurate than left/right
∫u dv = uv - ∫v du; derived from the product rule: d(uv) = u dv + v du
A function is differentiable at a point if the derivative exists there; requires continuity and a smooth curve (no corners, cusps, or vertical tangents)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Differentiability" is defined as: A function is differentiable at a point if the derivative exists there; requires continuity and a smooth curve (no corners, cusps, or vertical tangents). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Notation"?

options.text: Derived from the limit definition using sin(x+h) = sin x cos h + cos x sin h and special limits
f'(x), dy/dx, d/dx[f(x)], Df(x), ẏ are all common notations for the derivative
Applying the power rule term-by-term: d/dx[3x⁴ - 2x² + 5x - 1] = 12x³ - 4x + 5
The set of input values that make physical sense in the context; endpoints may produce absolute extrema

quiz_questions.explanation: "Notation" is defined as: f'(x), dy/dx, d/dx[f(x)], Df(x), ẏ are all common notations for the derivative. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "f'(x) = lim(h→0) [f(x+h) - f(x)]/h; the instantaneous rate of change of f with respect to x"?

options.text: Free response strategy
Derivative definition
Net change interpretation
Removable discontinuity

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Derivative definition".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "[f(x+h) - f(x)]/h; the average rate of change over an interval of width h; becomes the derivative as h→0"?

options.text: Hypothesis checking
Total vs. net
Common quotient rule errors
Difference quotient

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Difference quotient".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The line that touches the curve at exactly one point locally and has slope equal to the derivative: y - f(a) = f'(a)(x - a)"?

options.text: Extreme Value Theorem
Second derivative
Tangent line
Restricting domain

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Tangent line".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A function is differentiable at a point if the derivative exists there; requires continuity and a smooth curve (no corners, cusps, or vertical tangents)"?

options.text: Differentiability
When the test fails
Speed vs. velocity
Lorenz curve

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Differentiability".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "f'(x), dy/dx, d/dx[f(x)], Df(x), ẏ are all common notations for the derivative"?

options.text: Surface area of revolution
Notation
Lorenz curve
One-sided limits

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Notation".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Introduction to Derivatives is accurate?

options.text: These three rules, combined with basic derivatives, enable differentiation of virtually any function.
Antiderivatives: Inverse Trigonometric Forms produces inverse trig functions from specific integrand patterns.
This yields the derivatives of inverse trigonometric functions: d/dx[arcsin x] = 1/√(1-x²), d/dx[arccos x] = -1/√(1-x²), d/dx[arctan x] = 1/(1+x²).
Introduction to Derivatives defines the derivative as the limit of the difference quotient: f'(x) = lim(h→0) [f(x+h) - f(x)]/h.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Introduction to Derivatives as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Introduction to Derivatives?

options.text: Derivatives represent rates of change in any context: population growth rates, velocity, temperature change rates, marginal economic quantities, and reaction rates.
Phase line analysis reveals stability: P = 0 is unstable (arrows point away), P = K is stable (arrows point toward).
Common scenarios include expanding/contracting shapes, Pythagorean relationships, trigonometric relationships, and similar triangles.
Geometrically, the derivative gives the slope of the tangent line to f(x) at a point.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Introduction to Derivatives as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Introduction to Derivatives?

options.text: Graphs also reveal asymptotic behavior and discontinuities.
Riemann sums approximate this area using rectangles: left, right, and midpoint sums with n subintervals.
The derivative function f'(x) represents the instantaneous rate of change of f at every point in its domain.
Basic Derivative Rules provide shortcuts for finding derivatives without using the limit definition every time.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Introduction to Derivatives as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Introduction to Derivatives?

options.text: A function is differentiable at a point only if it is continuous there (but continuity alone does not guarantee differentiability—corners, cusps, and vertical tangents are non-differentiable).
U-Substitution (Part 2): Advanced Techniques handles more complex substitution scenarios.
As n→∞, Riemann sums converge to the definite integral: ∫ₐᵇf(x)dx = lim(n→∞) Σf(xᵢ*)Δx.
Key consequences include: (1) if f'(x) = 0 for all x in (a,b), then f is constant, (2) if f'(x) = g'(x) for all x, then f(x) = g(x) + C, (3) if f'(x) > 0 on (a,b), then f is strictly increasing.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Introduction to Derivatives as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Introduction to Derivatives?

options.text: Introduction to Derivatives defines the derivative as the limit of the difference quotient: f'(x) = lim(h→0) [f(x+h) - f(x)]/h.
Arc Length and Surface Area uses integration to measure curve length and rotational surface area.
For logarithms: d/dx[ln x] = 1/x and d/dx[logₐ x] = 1/(x ln a).
Riemann sums approximate this area using rectangles: left, right, and midpoint sums with n subintervals.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Introduction to Derivatives as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Introduction to Derivatives?

options.text: Velocity as the derivative of position: v(t) = s'(t) tells you the instantaneous speed and direction of a moving object at any moment
Electrical engineering: integrating sinusoidal voltage V(t) = V₀sin(ωt) across a capacitor to find charge: Q = ∫V dt = -(V₀/ω)cos(ωt) + C
Marathon pace strategy: analyzing a runner's velocity function to identify periods of acceleration (increasing velocity) and deceleration (decreasing velocity)
Coulomb's law: electric force F = kq₁q₂/r² → ∞ as distance r → 0, creating a vertical asymptote that reflects the impossibility of zero separation between charges

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Introduction to Derivatives.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Introduction to Derivatives?

options.text: Estimating total rainfall from rate-of-rainfall data: ∫(rainfall rate)dt gives total accumulated precipitation over a storm event
AP Calculus free-response questions consistently test the ability to set up integrals from verbal descriptions and geometric reasoning
Marginal cost in economics: the derivative of the cost function C'(x) gives the cost of producing one additional unit, guiding optimal production decisions
Structural load analysis: EVT guarantees the existence of a maximum stress point on a continuous beam, ensuring engineers can find and design for worst-case loads

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Introduction to Derivatives.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Introduction to Derivatives applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: AP Calculus free-response questions consistently test the ability to set up integrals from verbal descriptions and geometric reasoning
Medical dosing: the rate of drug absorption into the bloodstream is modeled as the derivative of drug concentration with respect to time
Population modeling: P(t) = P₀e^(rt), so P'(t) = rP₀e^(rt) = rP(t), showing exponential growth rate is proportional to population size
Disease spread: the SIR model's infection curve resembles logistic growth, with the inflection point marking peak new infections

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Introduction to Derivatives.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Introduction to Derivatives?

options.text: Cooling/heating: Newton's law of cooling dT/dt = -k(T-Tₛ) is separable, giving T(t) = Tₛ + (T₀-Tₛ)e^(-kt)
Computer algebra systems: software like Wolfram Alpha uses algorithmic decision trees similar to this strategy to compute symbolic integrals automatically
Long-term drug concentration models: horizontal asymptotes represent the steady-state drug level that the body approaches with regular dosing
Velocity as the derivative of position: v(t) = s'(t) tells you the instantaneous speed and direction of a moving object at any moment

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Introduction to Derivatives.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Introduction to Derivatives in practice?

options.text: Control systems engineering: inverse tangent functions model phase shifts in frequency response, with derivatives indicating sensitivity to frequency changes
Marginal cost in economics: the derivative of the cost function C'(x) gives the cost of producing one additional unit, guiding optimal production decisions
Water tank problems: given inflow rate and outflow rate functions, compute water level at any time t and find when the tank is fullest
Species population modeling: deer populations in managed forests follow logistic growth, with K determined by food and habitat availability

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Introduction to Derivatives.
